message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 23
Recent changes: Green phase added NO production code — "dragon sword ench 9 → 400" confirmed the 50% clause wins over dragon when both apply (ench 9 >= 8 already triggers 50%).

Refactor while keeping all tests green. Evaluate naming first, then Four Rules, APP mass before/after. No code changed; confirm cleanliness or make a small justified improvement only. Do not implement ahead (dragon-only full reimbursement, cap exhaustion across claims, error cases not yet tested — note the NEXT test is dragon ench 5 → full reimbursement, which WILL require dragon logic, but that's the next cycle).

Run autonomously, return after completion.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
All 23 tests pass. The Green phase added no production code (the ench-9 test was already satisfied by the existing `enchantment >= 8` clause). Now I'll evaluate the code for refactoring opportunities.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

The most recent confirmed behavior is the interaction between the dragon material and high-enchantment clauses on claims. Let me look closely at the claim-related names, since that's where the latest test exercised behavior.

Key claim functions:
- `isHighlyEnchanted` — checks `enchantment >= 8`. **This is the critical name to evaluate.** The constant is named `HIGH_DAMAGE_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD` (8), distinct from `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD` (5, used in premium surcharges). The name `isHighlyEnchanted` is generic and does not convey *which* threshold or *what effect* it has. Given the ench-9 test just confirmed this clause triggers the 50% reimbursement, the function's purpose is specifically "does this item qualify for the half (50%) reimbursement clause."
- `reimbursableAmount` — computes the reimbursable portion before deductible. Clear.
- `damagePayout` — clear.
- `claimResult` — clear.

**Assessment of `isHighlyEnchanted`:**
- Current name: `isHighlyEnchanted`
- Purpose: determines whether the half-reimbursement clause applies (enchantment ≥ 8)
- Question: Does `isHighlyEnchanted` reveal intent? Partially — it says "highly enchanted" but not that this triggers the *50% reimbursement* clause. It's also easily confused with the premium-side `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD` (5) concept.
- The ench-9 test just made it explicit: this clause is the one that "wins" by halving the payout. Renaming to `qualifiesForHalfReimbursement` ties the predicate directly to its single consumer (`reimbursableAmount`) and to the `HALF_REIMBURSEMENT_RATE`, removing ambiguity with the unrelated premium high-enchantment surcharge.

Decision: **Rename `isHighlyEnchanted` → `qualifiesForHalfReimbursement`.** This is a Rule 2 (reveals intent) improvement — it disambiguates the two "high enchantment" concepts and names the predicate by its effect rather than a vague adjective.

## Step 2: Calculate Initial APP Mass (affected functions)

Renaming does not change APP mass — it's a pure clarity improvement. Mass for the affected lines:

```
const isHighlyEnchanted = (item: Item | undefined): boolean =>
  (item?.enchantment ?? 0) >= HIGH_DAMAGE_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD;

- Bindings: isHighlyEnchanted, item, item?.enchantment, HIGH_DAMAGE_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD = 4
- Constants: 0 (in ??) = 1
- Invocations: ?? , >= → ~2 operators = 4
- Conditionals: 0
Total local mass ≈ 9
```

A rename leaves all counts identical: **9 → 9 (Δ 0)**. The improvement is purely Rule 2.

## Step 3–4: Evaluate the other rules and apply the change

- **Rule 1 (Tests Pass):** Green now. Will re-run after change.
- **Rule 2 (Reveals Intent):** The rename above. Applying it.
- **Rule 3 (No Duplication):** No duplication introduced. Note the two threshold constants (5 and 8) are genuinely different domain concepts (premium surcharge vs. claim reimbursement) — not duplication; keeping both is correct.
- **Rule 4 (Fewest Elements):** No unnecessary elements. I will **not** add dragon-only logic (next cycle's `dragon ench 5 → full reimbursement` test) — implementing ahead is prohibited.

Applying the rename. The identifier is used in two places (definition and call site in `reimbursableAmount`).

content.text:
All 23 tests still pass.

## Refactoring Complete

**Refactoring applied (one change):** Renamed `isHighlyEnchanted` → `qualifiesForHalfReimbursement` in `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_06-28-45_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.5-end-refactor_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts` (definition + its single call site in `reimbursableAmount`).

**Why (Naming first, Rule 2 — Reveals Intent):** The ench-9 test just confirmed this predicate is precisely the clause that halves the payout when both the dragon and high-enchantment clauses apply. The old name `isHighlyEnchanted` was a vague adjective and, worse, collided conceptually with the premium-side `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD` (5) — a different domain rule. The new name ties the predicate to its actual effect (the 50% / `HALF_REIMBURSEMENT_RATE` reimbursement) and removes that ambiguity. Naming the predicate by effect rather than by a feature description is the higher-clarity choice now that its role is pinned down by tests.

**Four Rules evaluation:**
- Rule 1 (Tests Pass): green before and after — 23 passed.
- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): improved via the rename.
- Rule 3 (No Duplication): none found. The two distinct thresholds (`HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD` = 5 for premium surcharge, `HIGH_DAMAGE_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD` = 8 for claim reimbursement) are separate domain rules, not duplication — correctly kept separate.
- Rule 4 (Fewest Elements): no superfluous elements; nothing to remove.

**APP mass:** Unchanged (a rename adds/removes no constants, bindings, invocations, conditionals, loops, or assignments). Local mass of the affected helper ≈ 9 → 9, Δ 0. The win is purely Rule 2 clarity, which correctly trumps APP here.

**Not implemented ahead (as instructed):** The next cycle's test is `dragon-material sword, ench 5, damage 800 → payout 700` (dragon full reimbursement, then deductible). That WILL require introducing dragon-material logic in the claim path — but it is the next Red/Green cycle, so no dragon-only branch was added now. Cap exhaustion across claims and the error cases also remain unimplemented by design.

**Tests:** All 23 passing.
